coredata.dc:description: Aqueous Zn-ion batteries show superior development prospects and competitiveness with high theoretical capacity, abundant reserves and low potential. However, the inhomogeneous electrodeposition of zinc anodes and zinc dendrite growth highly limit their commercialization. To address these issues, multifunctional hybridized interfaces consisting of layered double hydroxide (LDH) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are constructed on the surface of zinc metal anodes. LDH with hydrophobicity will effectively shield the corrosion of aqueous electrolyte on the zinc anode, and simultaneously serve as a mechanical skeleton for zinc deposition. The hydrophilic GQDs will control the coordination environment of solvated Zn2+, reduce the reactivity of water and promote the uniform deposition of zinc ions along the (002) crystal surface. The interfacially modified Zn anode achieves more than 1000 h and 1200 h of stable cycling at 1 and 2 mA cm−2, respectively. Moreover, the assembled Zn@LDH@GQDs//NH4V4O10 full cells achieve 2000 stable cycles at a high current density of 10 A g−1. The present work reveals the intrinsic mechanism of inhibiting zinc dendrites by the protective layer of multifunctional hybrid materials, which provides an important idea for stabilizing zinc anodes.

head.ce:title: Construction of hydrophilic and hydrophobic hybrid interface to achieve controlled zinc deposition for aqueous Zn-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Sustained and Advanced Functional Materials, College of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aqueous Zn-ion batteries show superior development prospects and competitiveness with high theoretical capacity, abundant reserves and low potential. However, the inhomogeneous electrodeposition of zinc anodes and zinc dendrite growth highly limit their commercialization. To address these issues, multifunctional hybridized interfaces consisting of layered double hydroxide (LDH) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are constructed on the surface of zinc metal anodes. LDH with hydrophobicity will effectively shield the corrosion of aqueous electrolyte on the zinc anode, and simultaneously serve as a mechanical skeleton for zinc deposition. The hydrophilic GQDs will control the coordination environment of solvated Zn2+, reduce the reactivity of water and promote the uniform deposition of zinc ions along the (002) crystal surface. The interfacially modified Zn anode achieves more than 1000 h and 1200 h of stable cycling at 1 and 2 mA cm−2, respectively. Moreover, the assembled Zn@LDH@GQDs//NH4V4O10 full cells achieve 2000 stable cycles at a high current density of 10 A g−1. The present work reveals the intrinsic mechanism of inhibiting zinc dendrites by the protective layer of multifunctional hybrid materials, which provides an important idea for stabilizing zinc anodes.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of science and technology, electric cars, portable electronic devices, and massive grid energy storage systems have been swiftly produced and widely employed. Therefore, human demand for safe, high-energy-density energy is expanding. Aqueous zinc ion batteries (ZIBs) have attracted much interests of researchers as one of the most promising alternatives because of their high theoretical specific capacity (820 mAh g−1), low cost, abundant sources, high safety, environmental friendliness, and relatively low redox potential (−0.762 V vs SHE) [1–6]. Unfortunately, rampant dendrite growth on zinc anodes, hydrogen precipitation due to thermodynamic instability, and unavoidable self-corrosion have seriously hindered the development of ZIBs [7–11]. In order to solve these problems, strategies such as optimization of electrolyte composition, structural design of zinc anode and interface modification have been successively proposed, among which interface modification is considered to be one of the best choices for the fabrication of stable zinc anodes [12–15]. The artificial interfacial layer, which resembles the solid electrolyte interface of a lithium anode, regulates the electrochemical environment on the anode surface and also plays the role of a physical barrier to keep the anode from directly contacting the electrolyte, greatly enhancing the cycling stability of the anode [16–19]. Additionally, there is a plentiful supply of materials that will be used as artificial interfacial layers, including Ag [20], CaCO3 [21], polyamide (PA) [22], et al. These materials have excellent cycling stability and can limit the development of zinc dendrites to some extent. For instance, Liu et al. utilized CeO2 with higher work function as a coating to fabricate a charge-enriched ohmic layer that effectively improved the reversibility of Zn coating/stripping [23]. Although inorganic coatings can modulate Zn2+ flux and inhibit the formation of dendrites, they often suffer from structural fracture and separation from the substrate due to their brittleness and poor adhesion to Zn [24–27]. Therefore, it is a great challenge to prevent side reactions from electrolyte contact with zinc anodes.

Fabrication of hydrophobic hybrid interface is an alternative approach to solve the problems. For example, Fei et al. designed a hydrophobic hybrid organic/inorganic interface consisting of polyvinylidene fluoride, silica and zinc trifluoro-methanesulfonate (PVDF-HFP) [28]. The hydrophobicity prevented side reactions, and the complexation and de-complexation in polymer chain segments efficiently allowed Zn2+ to pass through the interface and be uniformly deposited on the zinc substrate. The resulting symmetric battery stably cycled for more than 900 h at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2. Although hydrophobic coatings effectively mitigate the interfacial side reactions induced by water, thereby reducing dendrite formation and increasing the coulombic efficiency (CE) of zinc anodes, the utilization of hydrophobic organic materials as an interfacial layer tends to induce substantial interfacial polarization, which is suboptimal for achieving stable battery cycling [29–33]. Thus, it is important to find an ideal protective layer that will skillfully combine hydrophobicity and hydrophilicity. The hydrophobicity will inhibit side reactions among free water molecules the zinc anodes by immobilizing them in the space between the electrolyte and the anode, while the hydrophilicity helps to control the co-ordination environment of the solvated Zn2+ and reduces the reactivity of the water, thus enhancing the corrosion resistance of zinc anodes.

Herein, a multifunctional hybrid interfacial layer has been successfully prepared, which prevents hydrogen precipitation corrosion and dendrite formation, and promotes uniform Zn2+ deposits to create a stable anode. The electrostatic adsorption between the positively charged Mg6Al2(CO3)(OH)16·4(H2O) (LDH) layers and the negatively charged graphene quantum dots (GQDs) enables the formation of a stable composite material protective layer on the zinc metal surface. The hydrophobic LDHs will successfully prevent excess water molecules from co-embedding to reach the Zn surface and cause corrosion, while the internal hydrophilic GQDs help to create a stable inner electric field for uniform deposition of Zn ions along the (002) crystal surface. The symmetric cell made of Zn@LDH@GQDs operates stably for more than 1200 h at 2 mA cm−2 because of this synergy. The resulted zinc anodes show superior multiplicative performance and a longer cycle life when matched with NH4V4O10 cathodes for the research of full cells.

Electrochemical cutting processes were used to produce GQDs. Graphite was typically used as anode and cathode in 1000 cc of deionized water and electrolyzed in a beaker for one week at 30 kV. The precipitate was then filtered through a microporous filtration membrane with a width of 50 mm and particle size of 0.45 μm. Oriented GQDs was produced after rotational evaporation and freeze-drying.

Zn@LDH@GQDs electrodes were made by using customary slurry coating techniques. A typical procedure involved combining commercial magnesium aluminum hydrotalcite (Macklin), GQDs, and carboxymethylcellulose (CMC), well powdered in a mass ratio of 6:2:2, in a deionized water solvent, to create a slurry. The combination was then applied as a paste to 100 μm thick zinc foil, and after 12 h of drying at 60 °C in a vacuum oven, Zn@LDH@GQDs electrodes were produced. (The mass ratio of LDH and CMC in the Zn@LDH electrode was 8:2, prepared as above.)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NH4V4O10 nanoplates cathode materials
ce:section.ce:para: It was simple to synthesize NH4V4O10 by a hydrothermal procedure. A typical procedure involved dissolving ammonium metavanadate (5 mmol, NH4VO3, Macklin) in 30 mL of distilled water and stirring it for 15 min at room temperature. The aforementioned solution received an additional 40 min of magnetic stirring after the addition of oxalic acid (2 mmol, H2C2O4, Macklin). The aforementioned solution was then put into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. The resulted precipitate was retrieved using a centrifuge, vacuum evaporated at 60 °C for one day, and at least several times washed with deionized water.

The full cell was assembled by using Zn@LDH@GQDs as the anode and NH4V4O10 as the cathode. Casting of NH4V4O10 cathode material on carbon paper by needle press method, and the NH4V4O10 electrode contained 70 wt% NH4V4O10 powder, 20 wt% carbon black and 10 wt% polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF). After that, a vacuum oven was used to dry the cathode material for 24 h at 80 °C with a mass load of around 1.5 mg cm−2. The electrolyte employed throughout the cell was a 2 M Zn(OTf)2 aqueous solution. Throughout the full cell test, there was around 100 μL of electrolyte. With a Neware cell test system with a voltage range of 0.3–1.7 V, constant current charge and discharge performance tests were conducted. The long-term cycling behavior was performed at 5 A g−1 and 10 A g−1. As a comparison, Zn@LDH and Zn foil were also used as anodes.

The materials were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, XL 30 ESEM-FEG, FEI Company), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, JEOL-2100F), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010F), nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZSE), fourier infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet iS5 FT-IR Spectrometer), X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku SmartLab X-ray diffractometer), atomic force microscope (AFM, Asylum MFP3D), contact angle (CA) measurements (CA, sl200kb) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, monochromatic Al Ka radiation) with an energy step size of 0.1 eV.

The calculations were performed using the Vienna Ab Initio Package (VASP) of first-principles calculations based on density functional theory (DFT). The core separation and valence electron interactions are described by projected added waves (PAW), and the local density is described using the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) based on exchange correlation energy PBE (Periodic Boundary Embedding). Brillouin zone divisions sampled using Monkhorst-Pack method. ENCUT= 400 eV is selected as the cutoff energy value for the calculation. The K points of dimensions 1 × 1*1–3 × 3*1 is generated by optimizing the convergence sampling in the calculation of the diffusion barrier and the mechanical strength, respectively. The constructed interface/interface model (including GQDs and LDH (100)) has a lattice mismatch rate of <5 % in both u and v directions, and in the vertical direction, a vacuum layer of at least 15 Å in thickness is introduced for all the surfaces and interfaces. During the calculations, the bottom one layer is fixed at their bulk positions, whereas the top two layers and all adsorbed species are relaxed. The adsorption energy (Eads) is given by: Eads = Ecomplex - (EA+EB+EZn/ H2O), where Ecomplex, EA, EB and EZn/H2O correspond to the total energy of the optimized system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposition of multifunctional hybridized protective layers
ce:section.ce:para: In order to induce the uniform deposition of Zn2+, inhibit the growth of Zn dendrites and stabilize the Zn metal anode, a multifunctional hybrid material protective layer based on hydrophobic LDH and hydrophilic GQDs composition is designed and fabricated (Fig. 1a ). The strong adsorption among GQDs and Zn2+ makes them a suitable choice for inducing Zn2+ deposition [34], and the Mg-Al LDH also shows excellent protection against Zn anodes [35]. Besides, the layer structure of the LDH is positively charged [36,37], so it will electrostatically adsorb negatively charged GQDs on the surface (Fig. S1) and make a uniform distribution of GQDs within it (Fig. 1b), as can be demonstrated by TEM (Fig. S2). The luminescence by Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope confirms the presence of GQDs (Fig. S3). Subsequently, valid and practical X-ray diffraction (XRD) tests are performed to expose differences in phase structure among designed and bare zinc electrodes (Fig. S4), where two peaks at Zn@LDH@GQDs at 11.6° and 23.4° are associated to the (003) and (006) crystal faces of LDH, and the peak at 26.3° is the characteristic peak of the carbon (002) crystal plane.

Additionally, the information of the modified Zn anode surface is also determined in combination with XPS (Fig. 1c). The modified Zn surface is covered by CC, CO, and OC = O structures, which correspond to the three peaks located at the positions of 284.58, 285.86, and 288.64 eV in the C 1 s spectrum, respectively [38]. It is observed that the relative peak area ratios of the CO bonds increase from 16.31 % to 46.03 % and those of the OC = O bonds increase from 9.36 % to 12.23 % after the introduction of GQDs. The increase in the content of oxygen-containing functional groups will enhance their interaction with the zinc substrate and promote the uniform deposition of Zn2+.

Magnesium aluminum hydroxide compound, also known as layered double hydroxides, is a class of compounds with a layered shape. Its basic structure is composed of M2+-OH, M3+-OH octahedra, where M2+ are divalent metal cations such as Mg2+ and Mn2+. M3+ are trivalent metal cations such as Al3+ and Fe3+, which may form positively charged laminates and also contain solventized molecules and charge compensation anions in the layer space [39]. In this study, a commercially available Mg-Al LDH is used, and the characteristic peaks shown in its XRD pattern (Fig. S5) are consistent with the rhombomorph Mg6Al2(CO3)(OH)16·4(H2O) (JCPDSNo. 89–0460). As Fig. 2a shows, both Mg2+ and Al3+ are located in the octahedral center in Mg-Al LDH, and OH− occupies the connected vertices, forming a two-dimensional infinite layer structure. Mg-Al LDH contains two different layer structures, because the presence of CO3 2− anions between the layers results in a slightly larger interlayer distance for the type I layer (7.70 Å) than for the type II layer (7.36 Å). However, it is obvious that either layer in the Mg-Al LDH is sufficient to accommodate hydrated Zn2+ with a diameter of about 5.5 Å. This fact makes Mg-Al LDH a potentially suitable host for easy Zn2+ transport.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of GQDs illustrated in Fig. 2b confirm the uniform distribution of GQDs. The great crystallinity of the GQDs is revealed by the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2c), indicating that graphite will be electrolyzed to create GQDs. Fig. 2d displays the size statistics of multiple points in various photos, with a mean particle size distribution of 2.48 nm. The XRD pattern of GQDs is shown in Fig. S6, where a peak at 26.3°is aligned with the standard card of carbon (JCPDS No. 41–1487). Fourier infrared (FT-IR) is used to identify the surface functional groups of GQDs, and Fig. S7 illustrates the abundance of functional groups containing oxygen on the surface of GQDs. In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements also clearly show that GQDs are rich in functional groups. In the C 1 s spectra, the peaks at 288.79, 286.43, and 284.77 eV are attributed to C = O, CO, and CC, respectively (Fig. S8a). Meanwhile, C = O and CO are also observed at 533.01 and 532.5 eV in the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S8b) [38].

The exposed zinc foil needs to be polished first to remove the oxidized layers formed on the surface. But the surface of the zinc foil will show many scratches after polishing (Fig. 2e). Due to coating with pure LDH, the zinc foil gets dense but at the same time the coating is not homogeneous (Fig. 2f). The distribution of LDH on the zinc foil surface becomes flatter and more uniform with the addition of GQDs, which improves LDH aggregation (Fig. 2g). The consistent distribution of Mg, Al, C and O in the composite protective layer is verified by the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elements mapping (Fig. 2h). Because interfacial wettability and charge dispersion are critical for the anodic protection layer, the hydrophilicity of electrodes is studied by measuring the contact angle with 2 M ZnSO4. The contact angle of bare Zn is approximately 72.3°, indicating the limited hydrophilicity of the Zn metal surface in aqueous environments. It is worth noting that the LDH@Zn foil exhibits a contact angle of 81.4°. The results demonstrate the higher hydrophobicity of the LDH coating compared to Zn foil, effectively inhibiting undesired side reactions. In stark contrast, Zn@LDH@GQDs display a reduced contact angle of 50.4°, indicating a significant enhancement in hydrophilicity attributed to the abundant polar functional groups present in GQDs. The enhanced hydrophilicity will lower the interfacial free energy between the zinc substrate and the electrolyte, resulting in uniform deposition of Zn (Fig. 2i–k).

In addition, the DFT (density functional theory) calculations depicted in Fig. S37 demonstrate the binding energy of H2O molecules on the Zn, LDH, and LDH@GQDs planes. It is worth noting that the LDH plane exhibits a higher binding energy for H2O molecules (−1.97 eV) compared to the Zn plane (−2.07 eV), providing additional evidence for the repulsion of water molecules by LDH. Considering that deposition of Zn ions at the electrode/electrolyte interface involves removing water from hydrated Zn ions, it is inferred that the introduction of LDH expedited removal of water from solvated Zn ions within the interface layer, thereby delaying hydrogen gas evolution and related side reactions.
Afterwards, the optimal thickness of the hybrid material coating on the Zn anode is about 25.1 μm (Fig. 2l) determined by CE (Fig. S9).

Benefit by the LDH@GQDs overlayer, the zinc corrosion induced by the side reactions between Zinc anode and electrolyte is mainly controlled. By comparing the surface form of bare Zn, Zn@LDH, and Zn@LDH@GQDs after 2 and 5 days of immersion in the electrolyte (Fig. S10a, b), it is observed that Zn@LDH@GQDs has better chemical stabilization in the aqueous electrolyte. The surface of Zn@LDH@GQDs also maintains its integrity even after the immersion time is extended to 5 days, with no obvious zinc dendrite formation. The pure LDH protective layer, on the other hand, exhibits wrinkling and flaking with a minor amount of Zn dendritic crystalline formation following immersion in the electrolyte, whereas bare Zn exhibits uncontrolled dendritic crystalline growth. In the meantime, XRD images of Zn@LDH@GQDs (Fig. S10c) reveal that the usual Zn sulfate hydrate by-products (Zn4SO4(OH)6•xH2O) from Zn corrosion are hardly present.

After that, an experiment of long time immersion of the pole piece is carried out. When an excess amount of LDH is added to water, it is clearly to see that LDH is not soluble in water at all (Fig. S11a), indicating that LDH is well stabilized in water and ideal for use as the main material to protect the zinc anode coating [39]. However, after immersing the LDH alone as a protective layer in the electrolyte for up to half a month, visible symptoms of unevenness and flaking on the surface of the Zn@LDH pole piece are visible (Fig. S11e). The obtained Zn@LDH@GQDs protective layer adheres firmly to the Zn substrate because of the strong interaction between the oxygen-containing functional groups in Zn and GQDs (Fig. S11d). The electrolyte remains clear and transparent after immersion, and the protective layer shows no signs of dissolution and peeling (Fig. S11b, c). The results show that the composite protective barrier is effective in preventing direct contact between the zinc anode and the electrolyte and reducing the development of undesirable interfacial by-products.

Zn//Zn, Zn@LDH//Zn@LDH, and Zn@LDH@GQDs//Zn@LDH@GQDs symmetric cells are constructed to study the deposition behavior of Zn. As seen in Fig. 3a , a flat and dense surface is observed on the Zn@LDH@GQDs substrate with a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2 and a control galvanic capacity of 2 mA h cm−2. However, when the galvanic capacity is increased to 5 mAh cm−2, the substrate still maintains its flatness, as opposed to the surface of the pure LDH protective layer, which exhibits signs of depression and unevenness. In comparison, the surface of the bare zinc anode displays a multitude of apparently sharp zinc dendrites. After removing the LDH@GQDs coating, a smooth and flat galvanized coating is seen (Fig. S12). At the same time, the cross-sectional images (Fig. S13) also confirm that the hybrid protective layer induces a uniform deposition of zinc ions. As the plating volume rises, the images show that the Zn@LDH@GQDs remain flat and tight, while the pure LDH protection layer becomes loose and gradually separates from the substrate.

Moreover, the thickness of the bare Zn anode changes significantly and there are vertical Zn plates appear on it. In the XRD patterns obtained after Zn deposition on different anodes at 2 mA cm−2 and 5 mA cm−2, it is observed that Zn@LDH@GQDs (2.51, 1.91) exhibits a higher I(002)/I(100) ratio compared to both Zn (1.11, 0.84) and Zn@LDH (1.81, 1.42), as depicted in Fig. 3b, c. This suggests that the preferred direction for Zn deposition with GQDs support is along the (002) crystal plane, commonly referred to as lateral deposition. Lateral deposition along the (002) plane tends to promote more uniform Zn deposition across the electrode surface. Uniform deposition reduces the formation of dendrites, which are a major cause of short circuits and battery failure and hence is favor for the improvement of the overall performance and stability of the electrodes.

Transverse deposition efficiently avoids the tip effect, prevents sharp Zn dendrites from growing vertically and puncturing the diaphragm, and causes the cell to fail. In addition, employing in situ optical microscopy, the morphological evolution and dynamic processes of zinc deposition are tracked in real time (Fig. S14).The prominent Zn deposits on the bare Zn surface progressively evolved to the notorious dendrites with increasing deposition time, whereas no clear dendrite growth is seen on the Zn@LDH@GQDs electrode surface, indicating that Zn2+ are preferentially deposited along the (002) plane for deposition. Most notably, the composite protective layer do not peel off during the continuous deposition, indicating that the protective layer has strong adhesion and volume expansion resistance.

Fig. S38 displays the simulations conducted using COMSOL Multiphysics to analyze the distribution of Zn-ion flux and current across various electrodes. It is observed that both the Zn-ion flux and current exhibit a high concentration at the protrusions present on the pristine Zn surface, indicating that localized polarization of Zn-ion concentration is induced by what is commonly referred to as the "tip effect". The accumulation and growth of dendrites in Zn ions are driven by the high charging occurring at the tip sites. In stark contrast, the Zn@LDH@GQDs electrode demonstrates a uniform distribution of Zn-ion concentration and electric field, indicating that the hydrophobic-zincophilic and elastic interface effectively maintains a balanced Zn-ion concentration and redistributes electric fields on the surface of the electrode, resulting in a compact and seamless deposition of Zn.

In addition to dendrite growth, interfacial side reactions also have a significant impact on cycle reversibility. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements of half-cells were used to further study the impact of the composite protective layer on the dissolution/plating behavior of zinc. The CV curves of the electrodes coated with the composite protective layer, as seen in Fig. S15, display similar reduction/oxidation pairs, demonstrating that the coating has no impact on the electrochemical process. Testing the three-electrode system's linear polarization Tafel curves allows researchers to study the rate of corrosion of Zn anodes (Fig. S16). The self-corrosion potential of Zn foil increases after covering the composite protective layer, showing an improved corrosion resistance. This is considered as a result of the addition of GQDs, which provides more electronegative functional groups on the anode surface and decreases the interaction of Zn foil with H2O and SO4 2− in the electrolyte.

To further investigate the morphological evolution of the Zn surface after 50 cycles, a non-in situ scanning electron microscopy (SEM) test was used to reveal the Zn deposition/dissolution behavior at a current density of 1 mA cm−2 for a plating capacity of 1 mAh cm−2. As shown in Fig. 4a1-a4, the bare Zn anode surface shows a disordered distribution of protruding Zn dendrites after 50 cycles, while the corresponding cross-sectional images also show uneven Zn deposition (Fig. S17a), which is caused by the presence of uneven tips on the Zn foil surface, resulting in charge accumulation and thus wild dendrite growth. In contrast, although no dendrite growth is visible on the surface after the application of a separate LDH protective layer, there is a large amount of glass fibers adhering to the Zn anode surface (Fig. 4b1) and the surface of the pole piece becomes uneven, as confirmed by the cross-sectional images (Fig. S17b).

Stripping the protective layer later reveals that the deposition on the Zn surface is uneven (Fig. 4b2-b4). The Zn@LDH@GQDs electrode, in contrast to the two electrodes stated above, maintains a flat, dense surface (Fig. 4c1) and no dendrite formation is seen following a continuous plating/stripping process. In order to verify the further protective effects of the hybrid material protective layer (Fig. 4c2-c4), the LDH@GQDs layer is removed from the Zn anode and the surface morphology is examined. As expected, the inclusion of GQDs generates uniform Zn2+ nucleation and deposition parallel to the Zn substrate, which is responsible for the absence of dendrite growth and uniform deposition on the Zn anode. The results of the atomic force microscopy (AFM) tests also demonstrate a more uniform deposition of zinc ions under the LDH@GQDs protective layer after 50 cycles (Fig. 4d ), while a protruding uneven surface will be seen on bare Zn and Zn@LDH (Fig. S18). In addition to morphological analyses, XRD tests are used to track the changes in crystal structure that occurred following cycling in all samples. In bare Zn and Zn@LDH, as seen in Fig. 4e, several diffraction peaks of Zn4SO4(OH)6-xH2O by-products are found, but Zn@LDH@GQDs demonstrates no appreciable by-product formation following cycling as in the original metallic Zn.

The Zn||Zn@LDH@GQDs cells were assembled to investigate the deposition behavior of Zn metal anode, and 2 mAh cm−2 Zn was deposited on the Zn@LDH@GQDs anode at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2. The intimate contact between the LDH@GQDs coating and Zn metal is clearly evident from the elemental mapping images (Fig. S35), showing a homogeneous distribution of Mg, Al, on the LDH@GQDs coating. No obvious presence of Zn element is detected on the coating layer, indicating that during the deposition process, Zn permeated through the artificial protective layer and deposited beneath the Zn@LDH coating. This depositional behavior serves to mitigate dendrite formation as well as effectively prevent direct contact between zinc and electrolyte, thereby reducing side reactions. The result of DFT calculation confirms this theory. As shown in Fig. S36, the binding energy of Zn atom on Zn (−0.89 eV) and that of Zn atom on LDH (−1.13 eV) is higher than that of Zn atom on GQDs (−1.87 eV), and the binding energy of Zn atom on LDH@GQDs is the lowest (−2.06 eV). The results indicate a higher affinity of the coating towards zinc.

Consequently, zinc atoms are more prone to migrate and infiltrate into the beneath of coating, which will facilitate a more uniform deposition of zinc beneath the coating, leading to the formation of an evenly distributed layer. This is consistent with the elemental mapping image of the Zn@LDH@GQDs electrode after depositing Zn metal.

The cross-sectional SEM images of Zn@LDH@GQDs exhibit a uniform stripping phenomenon with flat surfaces, as shown in Fig. S39a. It is crucial to effectively illustrate the flatness of the Zn@LDH@GQDs subsequent to Zn stripping, thus cross-sectional SEM images (Fig. S39b) were examined after partial removal of the protective layer. Evidently, both the unremoved protective layer and exposed Zn foil exhibit a flat morphology. In comparison, craggy features are observed on the surfaces of stripped Zn, as depicted in Fig. S39c. The flat stripping interface can effectively mitigate the localized deposition of zinc ions and minimize the risk of zinc dendrite growth.

Subsequently, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements are performed in Zn symmetric cells with and without a composite protective layer to investigate the kinetics of Zn2+ plating/exfoliation. As illustrated in Fig. 4f, covering the Zn foil with LDH@GQDs results in a decreased initial resistance for the Zn anode. More importantly, the resistance of the Zn@LDH@GQDs anode is still the lowest among the three anodes after either 30 (Fig. S19a) or 50 cycles (Fig. S19b) of the symmetric cell, indicating its fast charge transfer capability, which is attributed to the abundant groups contained in GQDs. Their presence effectively promotes charge transfer, and in addition, the ultra-fine nanostructure of GQDs will make a closer contact between electrolyte and electrode.

The long-term constant current cycling experiments in Zn//Zn symmetric cells with different protective layers is performed to validate the increase in battery electrochemical performance by the composite protective layer. Because continuous dissolution of CMC might result in uneven or flaking of the electrode surface, which will impair cell electrochemical performance [53]. Therefore, a Coulometric efficiency test wass used to determine the optimal amount of CMC (Fig. S20).

The battery assembly: Zn@LDH@GQDs, Zn@LDH, Zn foil was employed as the positive electrodes in Zn@LDH@GQDs//Zn@LDH@GQDs symmetric battery, Zn@LDH///Zn@LDH symmetric battery, Zn//Zn symmetric battery. The negative electrodes consisted of Zn@LDH@GQDs, Zn@LDH and Zn foil. A diaphragm, gasket, and shrapnel served as fillers. Finally, the battery was assembled using a CR2032 battery case with 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte. As illustrated in Fig. 5a , after 130 h of cycling at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2, the potential of the bare Zn symmetric cell unexpectedly falls due to insane side reactions and rampant dendritic formation, which is ascribed to a “soft short”. The Zn@LDH@GQDs//Zn@LDH@GQDs symmetric cells, on the other hand, may run stably at low overpotentials for more than 1000 h. The lower overpotential is attributed to the uniform Zn deposition without dendrites and the lower interfacial resistance. Moreover, the content of GQDs in the composite protective layer influences the properties of Zinc electrodes, which is optimal when the content of GQDs is 20 %, and the stable operation time exceeds that of the pure LDH coating by more than 600 h and that of the Zn@LDH@GQDs-40 % by more than 300 h (Fig. S21a), illustrating the effective development of the composite protective layer.

At an enhanced current density of 2 mA cm−2, the Zn@LDH@GQDs-20 % symmetric cell maintains a reversible deposition/dissolution process for more than 1200 h without noticeable variations (Fig. 5b), outperforming the protective layer without GQDs and the Zn@LDH@GQDs-40 % protective layer (Fig. S21b). Excessive GQDs content increases the surface tension of the poles, and leads to unevenness of the poles, which may be the main reason for the degradation of the electrochemical performance of the cells. Moreover, the Zn@LDH@GQDs symmetric cell still cycles stably for 500 h at a high current density of 4 mA cm−2 (Fig. S22). Following that, the multiplicative performance of Zn symmetric cells with and without a protective layer of GQDs is investigated. The electrodes with added GQDs consistently exhibit stable voltage polarization, as shown in Fig. 5c, and notably, the Zn@LDH@GQDs electrodes provide steadily increasing voltage hysteresis of 0.029, 0.032, 0.035, 0.042 and 0.057 mV when the current density is gradually increased from 0.2 mA cm−2 to 5.0 mA cm−2, respectively.

More remarkably, when the current density drops to 1 mA cm−2 after 100 cycles, the symmetric cell generally maintains a non-shrinking overpotential of 0.031 mV for over 800 h. Fig. S23 clearly shows that when the current density is raised to 2 mA cm−2, the bare Zn symmetric cell already exhibits unstable voltage fluctuations and short circuits. Surprisingly, when the adhesive CMC is replaced with PVDF, the Zn@LDH@GQDs anode still has excellent cycle life (Fig. S24), which proves the universality of the method in this work. The above results of the experiment indicate that the composite protective layer created with the inclusion of GQDs has improved cycle stability and rate capability when compared to the pure LDH protective layer. In comparison to prior investigations (Table S1), the excellent cycle life of the Zn@LDH@GQDs anode will also be established (Fig. 5d). This excellent performance exemplifies the double synergy of LDH in the inhibition of dendrite growth and GQDs in limiting Zn2+ ion deposition, resulting in not only a low overpotential but also a long cycle life at different current densities.

Furthermore, chronoamperometric (CA) measurements were performed at an overpotential of −150 mV to investigate the Zn deposition behavior. According to Fig. 5e, the bare Zn anode's current density gradually increases over a 400-second period, indicating that Zn2+ displays a protracted and irregular two-dimensional diffusion pattern during the deposition process, which results in the vertical enrichment of Zn at the tip and the production of numerous Zn dendrites. Compared to the pure LDH-coated Zn anodes, Zn@LDH@GQDs shows a long three-dimensional dense diffusion process following a fast two-dimensional diffusion process (20 s), indicating that Zn2+ is modulated to the initial adsorption sites that facilitates the formation of fine and abundant nucleation sites. Meanwhile, the migration number (
t
Z
n
2 +
) obtained by EIS before and after polarization shows that the Zn@LDH@GQDs protective layer is more favorable for Zn2+ ion transport. The LDH@GQDs coating has a
t
Z
n
2 +
of 0.80, which is greater than the values of pure CA (0.42) and bare Zn (0.25) (Figs. S25–S27). There are several reasons for the enhancement of the migration number of Zn@LDH@GQDs: GQDs possess a substantial abundance of oxygen functional groups on their surfaces in Fig. S8. Shown in Fig. S36, the binding energy of Zn atom on Zn (−0.89 eV) and that of Zn atom on LDH (−1.13 eV) is higher than that of Zn atom on GQDs (−1.87 eV). The results indicate a higher affinity of the coating towards GQDs. Oxygen-containing functional groups serve as active sites for Zn²⁺ ion adsorption and transport, consequently enhancing cation mobility. Show in Fig. S38, the interplay between LDH and GQDs can create synergistic effects that further enhance ion transport and stability. The combination of LDH's ion-intercalation properties with GQDs’ conductivity can lead to faster and more efficient Zn²⁺ ion transport.

The nucleation overpotential is examined using a Zn//Cu asymmetric cell to further support the superiority of the composite protective layer. The difference between the tip potential and the following plane potential, which represents the energy potential barrier during the initial deposition, is used to determine the nucleation overpotential's value. With a current density of 2 mA cm−2 and a plating capacity of 1 mA h cm−2, the nucleation overpotential of Zn@LDH@GQDs (19.3 mV) is much lower than that of Zn@LDH (36 mV) and bare Zn (39.7 mV) (Fig. 5f). This is as a result of the fact that GQDs with negatively charged functional groups will reduce the barrier to nucleation and produce additional active nucleation sites.

More researches are done on the long-term Zn plating/stripping coulomb efficiency (CE) of Zn//Cu asymmetric cells. Zn@LDH@GQDs demonstrates a very stable CE over 900 cycles with an average CE of 99.6 % after repeated plating/stripping at 2 mA cm−2, which is a significant improvement compared to Zn@LDH and bare Zn (Fig. 5g). Moreover, as can be seen in the inset of Fig. 5g, the voltage profile of Zn@LDH@GQDs at various cycles consistently maintains a lower plating/stripping voltage hysteresis than that of bare zinc. The Zn@LDH@GQDs electrodes demonstrate outstanding rate performance and excellent cycle stability at higher current densities, as shown in Fig. S28, with an average CE of 99.2 % at a current density of 4 mA cm−2 and a plating capacity of 1 mA h cm−2. By comparison, the CE of Zn@LDH and bare Zn shows severe swings and fail entirely after only over 100 cycles. There is no doubt that Zn@LDH@GQDs-20 % is more stable for long time plating/stripping of zinc ions than Zn@LDH@GQDs-40 % which contains excess GQDs (Fig. S29).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical tests of aqueous zinc batteries
ce:section.ce:para: The impedance spectrum in Fig. 6b shows that the Rct of Zn@LDH@GQDs//NH4V4O10 cells is smaller before and after cycling than that of bare Zn//NH4V4O10 and Zn@LDH//NH4V4O10 cells, confirming that Zn2+ has better translocation ability in fast redox reactions, which is consistent with our previous results in symmetric cells. These findings suggest that the composite coating may effectively minimize interfacial parasitic reactions, owing to its ability to avoid excessive contact between the electrolyte and the Zn anode and reject the entrance of SO4 2−. The Raman spectrum of LDH@GQDs (Fig. S33) shows two peaks near 1347 and 1590 cm−1 for the defect-related D-band and graphitic G-band, respectively. LDH@GQDs clearly contains more amorphous carbon than GQDs and LDH due to the fact that the ratio of D-band to G-band (1.02) is higher than that of GQDs and LDH alone. According to the higher value of ID/IG, the inclusion of GQDs enhances the active site of LDH, which is advantageous for the improvement of capacity.

Fig. 6c depicts the cycling stability of the full cell at various cycle times. After 1000 cycles at a current density of 5 Ag−1, the Zn@LDH@GQDs//NH4V4O10 full cell has a superior capacity of 265.3 mAh g−1 with a retention rate of 97.9 %. In comparison, bare Zn fails after just over 400 cycles due to significant Zn dendrite development and parasitic reactions, while the Zn@LDH//NH4V4O10 complete cell retains just 82.2 % of its capacity. The rate capability and cycle reversibility of the full cell are then tested at different current densities ranging from 1∼10 A g−1. As demonstrated in Fig. S34c, the Zn@LDH@GQDs//NH4V4O10 cell gives stable discharge capacities of 358.6, 326.3, 304.5, 268.4, 227.7 and 204.4 mAh g−1 when the current densities are 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, and 10 A g−1, respectively. As the current density decreases to 1 A g−1 after 60 cycles, a larger capacity of 379.7 A g−1 is still accessible with steady cycling capabilities. while the specific capacities of 370.5, 329.7, 301.2, 260.6, 220.2 and 194.4 mAh g−1 is achieved by Zn@LDH//NH4V4O10 at the same current densities of cycling, respectively (Fig. S34b). And Zn//NH4V4O10 full cells reach specific capacities of 356.6, 325.2, 301.4, 264.5, 224.6 and 201.3 mAh g−1, respectively (Fig. S34a). The Zn@LDH@GQDs//NH4V4O10 cell appears to have a greater capacity than the Zn@LDH//NH4V4O10 and Zn//NH4V4O10, indicating that the Zn@LDH@GQDs anode reduces side reactions and enhances durability.

In addition, as the current density is raised to 10 A g−1, Zn@LDH@GQDs// NH4V4O10 still exhibits a high discharge capacity of 237.5 mAh g−1 with a capacity retention of 98.1 % after 2000 cycles (Fig. 6e). In comparison, the capacity retention for the Zn@LDH//NH4V4O10 full cell is only 85.8 %. Due to significant Zn dendrite growth and parasitic reactions, the Zn//NH4V4O10 full cell fails after just 1200 cycles. The durability of the LDH@GQDs coating in the whole cell is confirmed further by studying the anode morphology after cycling. As Fig. 6f shows, the surface of the bare Zn anode has a considerable number of uneven dead Zn and severe corrosion, whereas the surface of the Zn@LDH anode has some glass fibers attached and becomes uneven. In comparison, the surface of the Zn@LDH@GQDs anode has no dendritic protrusions on its surface and is generally flat. The present results completely demonstrate the benefits of the Zn@LDH anode and its enormous potential for the production of exceptionally stable aqueous Zn-ion batteries. We assembled Zn@LDH@GQDs//NH4V4O10 pouch cell with an electrode area of 3 × 5 cm−2 that casting of 10.08 mg NH4V4O10 cathode material on carbon paper by needle press method. The pouch cell gives a capacity retention of 93 % for over 66 cycles at 1 A g−1 (Fig. S39a). After 700 cycles, the pouch cell with Zn@LDH@GQDs//NH4V4O10 shows no obvious swelling (Fig. S39b). The LDH@GQDs coating was found to effectively suppress the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) occurring on the anode side. The practical application of Zn@LDH@GQDs electrode has been demonstrated.

In conclusion, a multifunctional hybrid material protective layer combining hydrophobic LDHs and hydrophilic GQDs is fabricated for stabilizing zinc anodes. Among them, the hydrophobic LDH acts as a physical barrier to inhibit corrosion caused by the zinc anode in direct contact with the electrolyte. In addition, the hydrophilic GQDs are useful for controlling the coordination environment of solventized Zn2+ and lowering water reactivity, which enhances the corrosion resistance of zinc anodes. Due to the synergistic effect of LDH and GQDs, the prepared coatings will induce uniform deposition of zinc and effectively inhibit the growth of zinc dendrites. Moreover, the symmetric cell assembled by Zn@LDH@GQDs electrodes has excellent cycling stability. Furthermore, the Zn@LDH@GQDs//NH4V4O10 full cell is capable of cycling 2000 cycles at a high current density of 10 A g−1 with a capacity retention of up to 98.1 %. This easy and effective method of preparing the protective layer may lead to more new research on multicomponent protective layers in the future.
